strap: A daily dispatch from the world you are entering

lead.title: The child's routine becomes the household's hardest hire, as the travelling nanny turns into a career of her own
lead.standfirst: Multi-residence families and burned-out live-in staff are together reshaping private childcare into rotating teams, and the pay is following the pressure.
lead.body:
For a generation, the private nanny was a single figure who lived where the family lived and moved when the family moved. That model is quietly breaking. Agencies serving ultra-high-net-worth households now report that the fastest-growing brief is not the live-in nanny at all but the rota nanny, a professional who works a fixed cycle, typically two weeks on followed by two weeks off, so that the child always has continuity of care while no single person carries the whole year alone. The change is being driven from two directions at once. Families increasingly run three or four residences and expect childcare to travel with them across time zones. At the same time, experienced candidates have started to refuse the classic single-nanny arrangement outright, having watched colleagues burn out inside it.

The money has moved with the pressure. In the strongest markets a senior nanny is no longer a modest domestic line. Switzerland now leads the world on pay, with packages reported around USD 144,000, and the United States follows close behind at roughly USD 120,000, rising in New York to elite packages of USD 150,000 to USD 180,000 and beyond. A senior rota position based in Dubai will often carry a package worth from 80,000 to more than 120,000 pounds once accommodation and flights are counted. These are not pin money figures. They sit alongside the salaries of the estate managers and private chefs who run the same houses, and they are negotiated with the same seriousness.

Above the nanny sits the governess, and here the household is buying something closer to education than to care. A newly qualified governess with a teaching qualification and one or two years in a private house earns from 35,000 to 45,000 pounds gross. With three to five years behind her the range moves to between 45,000 and 65,000 pounds. The senior governess who is genuinely bilingual and willing to live out of a suitcase across several countries commands 65,000 pounds and upward, frequently past 90,000. What the family is paying for is fluency, discretion and the ability to hold a child's schooling together while the household itself is in motion.

For the person who runs the house, this is a structural shift rather than a passing fashion. A rota model means two or three childcare professionals per child rather than one, each with a contract, a travel schedule, a handover protocol and a claim on the family calendar. The house manager becomes the person who designs those cycles, keeps the handovers seamless and makes sure that the child never feels the seam between one carer leaving and the next arriving. The nursery has become, in effect, a small department, and someone has to run it.
lead.why: Childcare is moving from a single trusted figure to a managed rotating team, and the house manager is increasingly the one who designs the cycle, writes the handover and holds the continuity together.

articles.title: The family office stops being run by the family, and the outside professional inherits the keys
articles.body:
The family office was born as an intimate thing, a trusted relative or a long-serving adviser looking after one household's fortune. It is becoming an institution. Deloitte's global research puts the assets managed by single family offices on a path to rise by roughly 73 per cent, from about USD 3.1 trillion today to some USD 5.4 trillion by 2030, a scale that no relative can steward on instinct alone. As the money grows, so does the machinery around it, and with the machinery comes the professional.

The most telling number is not about assets but about who is in charge. Today only around 35 per cent of family office heads are non-family professionals. After the coming wave of generational handovers, Deloitte expects that figure to reach close to 49 per cent, meaning that in roughly half of these houses the person setting strategy will no longer share the family's surname. Four in ten family offices say they intend to add staff, and a growing share of that hiring is aimed squarely at outside expertise rather than promotion from within the family circle.

That professionalisation reshapes the household as a place to work. A family office run by an outside chief executive brings governance, written policies, defined reporting lines and human resources discipline into a world that used to run on personal loyalty and unspoken understanding. For the private staff who sit near the family, the house manager, the principal's assistant, the estate team, this can be steadying. Roles are written down, pay is benchmarked and progression becomes legible. It can also be colder. The handshake is replaced by the contract, and the person who once answered only to the principal now answers to a structure.

The direction of travel is clear enough. The private household is being absorbed into something that looks more like a small institution each year, with the family office as its head office and the residences as its operations. Whoever serves the family will increasingly serve it through that structure, and the ability to work inside a professional chain of command, not merely to please one person, becomes part of the craft.
articles.why: As outside executives take charge of family offices, private staff increasingly report into professional structures rather than to the principal alone, and fluency in governance becomes as valuable as personal trust.
articles.src: Deloitte Private, Family Office Insights Series

articles.title: The residence now arrives with a name on the door, and service is written into the deeds
articles.body:
One of the clearest signals of how the very wealthy now want to live is the branded residence, a private home attached to a hotel or fashion name and sold with that name's service built in. Savills counts the number of branded residence schemes rising from 764 at the end of 2024 to around 910 by the close of 2025, a growth rate of about 19 per cent in a single year. With 837 further projects contracted through 2032, the total pipeline points toward some 1,747 schemes. The map is widening with the numbers, spanning more than 90 countries, of which 25 are seeing their first branded development.

Buyers pay handsomely for the badge. A branded home carries a global average price premium of about 33 per cent over comparable unbranded stock, rising to roughly 39 per cent in resort locations. Part of that premium is prestige, but a large part is service. At the top of the market the names doing the defining are the ones a private principal already knows, Aman, Auberge, Montage and Kerzner in the ultra-luxury tier, with Four Seasons the single most influential operator, followed closely by Mandarin Oriental and Aman. Marriott and Accor lead on sheer scale, each carrying portfolios of more than 35 brands.

What the buyer is really purchasing is a household that comes pre-staffed. Housekeeping, security, maintenance and concierge arrive with the deeds, delivered to a hotel standard and charged through service fees. For a time-poor family with several homes, that is the appeal, a residence that runs itself the moment they walk in and keeps running when they leave.

For the private service profession this cuts two ways. The branded residence can absorb functions that a family once hired directly, thinning the traditional in-house team in favour of a building's shared staff. It also creates a large new field of employment inside those buildings, and it raises the bar for the private house manager who still runs a family's own estate, because the family now measures home against the seamless standard of a five-star operator, and expects to feel no difference.
articles.why: Branded residences fold housekeeping, security and concierge into the building, reshaping where private staff are employed and setting a hotel-grade benchmark that in-house teams are now judged against.

articles.title: Private flying sets fresh records, and the new fortune coming out of the markets is doing the flying
articles.body:
Private aviation has stopped being described as a recovery and is now simply setting records. Charter and fractional flight totals for 2026 are running about 42 per cent above their 2019 level, and the momentum has not faded with the summer. Late in July, a 12 per cent year-on-year jump in United States private jet departures lifted worldwide flights to a roughly 7 per cent gain, with the flight-tracking analysts at WingX noting that the four-week trend of about 7 per cent is outpacing the global year-to-date figure of around 5 per cent. Growth is showing up across every category at once, from owner flights to fractional ownership to on-demand charter.

The engine behind the numbers is new wealth. Analysts point to a surge of initial public offerings and a growing population of ultra-high-net-worth individuals as the clearest drivers of demand, with Jefferies reporting that 2026 flotation proceeds are running at nearly three times the level of the previous year. When a founder or an early employee turns paper worth into cash, one of the first purchases is mobility, and the correlation between flotation activity and jet deliveries is strong. Fresh money quite literally takes to the air.

The operators are building for it. NetJets, the largest fractional operator, has said it has pre-sold more than half of the aircraft deliveries it expects next year, and plans to take on the order of 100 aircraft this year and again the year after. That is a company betting real capital that the demand is structural rather than a passing spike, and its order commitments ripple through the whole manufacturing chain.

For the concierge and the house manager, this is the practical texture of the job. A principal whose wealth is newly liquid flies more, plans later and expects the machine on the tarmac when the diary shifts. Managing that means holding relationships with fractional providers and charter brokers, understanding lead times that lengthen as the fleet fills, and treating aviation as a standing logistical discipline rather than an occasional booking.
articles.why: New wealth from public offerings is flying more and planning later, which puts a premium on the house manager who treats aviation as a standing relationship with operators rather than a one-off booking.

articles.title: Fine wine settles at the floor, and the patient cellar becomes a buyer's advantage
articles.body:
After the euphoria of the early decade, fine wine has spent two years coming back to earth, and 2026 is the year it has settled there. The Liv-ex Fine Wine Investables Index, which tracks the blue-chip end of the cellar, sits roughly 25 per cent below its 2022 peak, with prices across many leading regions and vintages hovering near five-year lows. Liv-ex itself expects the market to bump along the bottom through the year, describing the moment not as a crash but as the start of a slow recovery, and openly framing it as an opening for buyers who were priced out at the top.

The correction has been broad. Bordeaux and Burgundy, the two anchors of any serious collection, have both given ground, and the speculative premium that once attached to the most fashionable Burgundy labels has thinned considerably. What remains valued, and what still trades, is provenance. In a soft market the buyer can afford to be exacting, and impeccable storage history and clean documentation have become the difference between a wine that sells and one that lingers. Condition is now the currency.

For the household this is less an investment note than a matter of cultural fluency and cellar management. A family cellar bought at the peak may be showing a paper loss, and the person who keeps the records should understand why without alarm. A patient household, by contrast, can build depth at prices unseen in years, provided it buys on provenance rather than label and stores the wine properly.

The craft point is quiet but real. Whoever runs the cellar is expected to know the market well enough to advise on when to hold, when to drink and when a bottle is worth insuring rather than opening, and to speak about it with the ease of someone who belongs in the room. In a buyer's market that fluency is worth more, not less.
articles.why: A soft fine wine market rewards the household that buys on provenance and stores with discipline, and it raises the value of a cellar keeper who can advise with genuine fluency rather than follow fashion.

briefs.b: Henley & Partners forecasts that as many as 165,000 millionaires will relocate in 2026, the largest wealth migration it has recorded. The United Arab Emirates remains the leading destination, with Singapore, Italy, Switzerland and Greece rising, while the United Kingdom, Germany and France are named among those losing ground.

Global auction sales at Christie's, Sotheby's and Phillips rose about 70 per cent year on year in the first half of 2026, to some USD 6.8 billion including fees, the best first half since 2022. Sotheby's reported total turnover of USD 4.4 billion, an all-time high, and luxury collectibles climbed 25 per cent, with memorabilia up a striking 308 per cent.

The formal butler remains one of the hardest roles to fill in London, with pay reported up by 20 to 25 per cent since 2022 and demand growing by about 9 per cent a year. Experienced housekeepers are described as genuinely scarce across the major markets, keeping the whole senior household team in a candidate's position.

BOAT International's Global Order Book lists 1,093 yachts in build or on order over 24 metres, with unit numbers down for a second straight year even as the average length under construction reaches a record 40.8 metres. Azimut Benetti tops the order book for a 26th successive year. The fleet is consolidating into fewer, larger vessels that demand larger and better-paid crews.
